title: Pruned vs. Unpruned Trees
concept: Pruning directly improves a model's ability to generalize by stripping away training-specific details, resulting in trees that are smaller, faster, and more accurate on new data.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a minimum samples requirement do when a branch grows small?

options.text: It stops the branch from splitting further if it has fewer data points than the minimum.
It forces the branch to double its size by copying data points.
It automatically prunes the root of the tree.

exercises.explain: Correct! Limiting the split size prevents the tree from asking questions for tiny, unreliable groups of data.

cards.title: The Over-Detailed Map
cards.body: Imagine opening a digital map of your city. An unpruned map displays every single tiny private alleyway, dirt path, and garden walkway. It is so cluttered you can't see the roads! A pruned map shows only major roads, highways, and useful streets. Cutting the unnecessary clutter makes the map readable and actually helps you navigate.

cards.title: Generalization
cards.body: Generalization is the ability of a machine learning model to make accurate predictions on new, unseen data. While an unpruned tree looks 'smarter' because it matches training data perfectly, a pruned tree generalizes far better because it focuses on general rules instead of training noise.

cards.title: The Custom Suit
cards.body: Imagine buying a custom suit tailored to fit you on a single day, with custom pockets designed to fit the exact amount of pocket change you had that afternoon. If you change what's in your pockets, the suit doesn't fit right! A standard tailored suit fits you on any day, under general conditions. Pruning turns the over-fitted suit into a general-purpose one.

cards.title: Weather Predictions
cards.body: An unpruned tree predicts rain because: 'It is Tuesday, humidity is 80%, and a blue bird flew past the window.' A pruned tree predicts rain because: 'Humidity is 80% and air pressure is falling.' Next Tuesday, when a red bird flies by instead, the pruned tree will still make the correct prediction, while the unpruned tree will fail.

cards.title: Simpler Models Can Be More Accurate
cards.body: You might think a massive, complex decision tree with thousands of branches must be highly accurate. In reality, simpler models are often more accurate in the real world. Simple, pruned trees are faster, easier to explain, and make fewer errors on new test data.

cards.title: Unpruned vs. Pruned Performance
cards.body: Comparing how pruning affects accuracy on new, unseen data:

left.text:
Training Accuracy: 100%
Test Accuracy: 65%
Overfit to noise.
Massive size, slow decisions.

right.text:
Training Accuracy: 90%
Test Accuracy: 88%
General rules.
Small size, fast decisions.

exercises.prompt: Why does a pruned decision tree typically perform better on new, unseen data than an unpruned tree?

options.text: It focuses on general, reliable patterns rather than memorizing noisy training details.
It has many more question levels to check every detail.
It converts the decision tree into a continuous linear regression line.
It requires the data points to be grouped in circular patterns.

exercises.explain: Correct! Pruning strips away training noise, which helps the tree focus on general patterns that actually carry over to new data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An unpruned tree is typically larger, deeper, and harder for humans to interpret than a pruned tree.
exercises.explain: True! Unpruned trees grow massive and messy with hundreds of specific branches, making them very difficult to read or understand.

exercises.sentence: Pruning improves a decision tree's ability to ___ to new, unseen data.
exercises.explain: Generalization is the ultimate goal in machine learning — making sure our model works in the real world, not just on our training examples.

exercises.prompt: Which of these test results suggests that pruning was successful?

options.text: The model's training accuracy dropped from 100% to 92%, but its accuracy on new test data rose from 70% to 85%.
The model's training accuracy stayed at 100%, and its test accuracy dropped to 50%.
The model's depth doubled, and its test accuracy remained the same.

exercises.explain: Exactly! A slight drop in training accuracy is normal, but the significant rise in test accuracy proves the model now generalizes much better to new data.

exercises.prompt: A sports team builds two decision trees to predict game winners. Model A has 150 split levels (unpruned). Model B is limited to 4 levels (pruned). When tested on next season's games, Model B predicts 85% of winners correctly, while Model A only gets 60% correct. Why did the simpler model win?

options.text: Model A memorized hyper-specific details of the past season's games (like what color socks they wore), while Model B learned general winning factors.
Model B was more complex and asked more detailed questions.
Unpruned models are always programmed to choose the wrong answers on purpose.
Model A did not have access to the player names.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Model A overfit by memorizing the noisy details of the training season. Model B, by being restricted and pruned, was forced to learn general rules that actually help predict future games. Congratulations! You've mastered tree pruning and regularization. Next, let's look at combining multiple trees into a forest!
